title: ActivityWatch vs Timeular for Minimalists
meta_description: See why Timeular fails first when tracking time requires interacting with a physical device to switch tasks instead of running passively in the background and why ActivityWatch is the better pick for minimalists.
constraint_lens: Minimalists need time tracking tools that run passively without requiring interaction or extra steps.

one_second_verdict.summary: Best for minimalists who want passive time tracking without interacting with devices.
one_second_verdict.reason: Timeular fails first because it requires interacting with a physical device to switch tasks instead of running passively.

verdict.summary: ActivityWatch is the better choice when you want time tracking to happen automatically. It runs in the background and tracks activity without requiring input. Timeular depends on physically switching a device to log tasks, which introduces friction and breaks passive tracking.
verdict.decision_rule: If tracking time requires interacting with a physical device to switch tasks instead of running passively in the background, Timeular fails first.

sections.heading: Why ActivityWatch fits this minimalist better
sections.content: This user wants tracking to happen without thinking about it. ActivityWatch supports that by running in the background and capturing activity automatically, removing the need for manual interaction.

bullets.point: ActivityWatch runs in the background and tracks app and device usage automatically.
bullets.why_it_matters: You do not need to start, stop, or switch tasks manually.

bullets.point: No physical interaction is required to record time.
bullets.why_it_matters: Tracking happens passively, which reduces effort and distraction.

bullets.point: Data is collected continuously without requiring user input.
bullets.why_it_matters: This ensures consistent tracking without relying on habits or reminders.

bullets.point: Timeular uses a physical device that you rotate to switch between tasks.
bullets.why_it_matters: This creates a clear signal for task changes, but requires manual interaction.

bullets.point: Tracking is tied to deliberate actions rather than automatic monitoring.
bullets.why_it_matters: This can improve intentional tracking, but adds effort.

bullets.point: The system provides a tangible way to represent tasks.
bullets.why_it_matters: This can feel engaging, but introduces an extra step for every switch.

sections.heading: Where each tool can break down

items.fails_when: You need precise control over task categories and intentional tracking of specific activities.
items.what_to_do_instead: Use Timeular if you prefer actively logging and switching tasks.

items.fails_when: You forget to switch the device or want tracking to happen without any interaction.
items.what_to_do_instead: Switch to ActivityWatch for fully passive tracking.

sections.heading: When this verdict might flip
sections.content: This can flip if the user prefers intentional tracking and wants a physical reminder to switch tasks. In that case, Timeular may be more effective.

sections.heading: Quick rules
rules: Choose ActivityWatch if you want passive tracking.
Choose Timeular if you want manual, intentional tracking.
If you want zero interaction, use ActivityWatch.

faqs.q: Why is ActivityWatch better for passive tracking?
faqs.a: Because it runs in the background and tracks activity automatically without user input.
It requires physically switching a device to log tasks.
It can be more intentional, but depends on remembering to switch tasks.

faqs.q: What is the best tool for zero-interaction tracking?
faqs.a: ActivityWatch is better because it requires no manual input.
